3A_Bibliographic.citation: Zink, J., Booker, R., Wolff-Hughes, D. L., Allen, N. B., Carnethon, M. R., Alexandria, S. J., & Berrigan, D. (2024). Longitudinal associations of screen time, physical activity, and sleep duration with body mass index in U.S. youth. International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity, 21, 35.
3A_Bibliographic.journal: International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Of the original 11,876 participants enrolled in the ABCD Study at baseline, 10,544 were included in the isotemporal substitution analytic sample. Many of these exclusions were due to missing BMI z-score at follow-up (n=777).
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Two analytic samples: isotemporal substitution (N=10,544) and CoDA (N=5,164).

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen time by modality (streaming, gaming, socializing) in minutes/day
3C_Exposure.modalities: streaming (TV/movies + YouTube), gaming (video games), socializing (texting + video chatting + social networking)
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous minutes/day; also analyzed as compositional proportions of day in CoDA
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Youth self-reported their screen time via the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey. Participants reported their usual time spent (hours per day) in six different forms of screen time on weekdays and weekend days separately. Weekday and weekend day reports were combined to generate the usual daily time spent in each form of screen time (hours); calculated as (screen time on weekdays*0.71) + (screen time on weekend days*0.29).

3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Anthropometric measurement (BMI calculated from measured height and weight, converted to z-scores using CDC 2000 Growth Charts)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: BMI (kg/m-2) was calculated based on height (cm) and weight (kg; measured in triplicate) measured by trained ABCD study staff. BMI was converted to z-scores using the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention 2000 Growth Chart SAS software, which generates metrics based on age and sex.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Isotemporal substitution linear regression and Compositional Data Analysis (CoDA); complex survey design with study site as cluster variable and ACS weights
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, race/ethnicity, socioeconomic status (income-to-needs ratio), internalizing symptoms, baseline BMI z-score
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Our primary analytical approach was isotemporal substitution to estimate the associations between replacing different forms of screen time, physical activity, and sleep with BMI. We selected 30-min substitutions. Linear regressions with all baseline behaviors, except the behavior being replaced were run. All analyses were sex-stratified. The study site was specified as the cluster variable and sample weights were applied to approximate the American Community Survey.

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Replacing 30 min of socializing with 30 min of physical activity (Females)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: In females, replacing 30 min of socializing (beta [95% CI] = -0.03 [-0.05, -0.002]), streaming (-0.03 [-0.05, -0.01]), or gaming (-0.03 [-0.06, -0.01]) with 30 min of physical activity was associated with a lower follow-up BMI z-score

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Replacing 30 min of streaming with 30 min of physical activity (Females)
Replacing 30 min of socializing with 30 min of sleep (Males)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: For males, replacing 30 min of socializing (beta [95% CI] = -0.03 [-0.05, -0.01]), streaming (-0.02 [-0.03, -0.01]), or gaming (-0.02 [-0.03, -0.01]) with 30 min of sleep was associated with a lower BMI z-score one year later.

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Replacing 30 min of socializing with 30 min of gaming (Males)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: replacing 30 min of socializing with 30 min of gaming was associated with a lower BMI z-score one year later (-0.01 [-0.02, -0.0001])

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Proportion of time in socializing relative to remaining behaviors (Males, CoDA)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: BMI z-score units per ILR unit
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: a greater proportion of time spent in baseline socializing, at the expense of time spent equally in the remaining behaviors, was associated with a higher BMI z-score at follow-up (beta [95% CI] = 0.05 [0.02, 0.08])

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: While the magnitude of the associations reported here are small and may not represent clinically meaningful associations with BMI across one year, our findings are important because of the pervasiveness of screen time worldwide and potential for these associations to accumulate over longer periods of time.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Uses asterisks for significance thresholds (*P<.05, **P<.01, ***P<.001). Reports CIs throughout.

3H_Recommendations.notes: The paper calls for experimental manipulation of screen time and investigation of biopsychosocial mechanisms but does not issue direct clinical or policy recommendations.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.population_override_verbatim: While the magnitude of the associations reported here are small and may not represent clinically meaningful associations with BMI across one year, our findings are important because of the pervasiveness of screen time worldwide and potential for these associations to accumulate over longer periods of time.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: modifiable behavioral risk factors to inform strategies for promoting healthy BMI in youth
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.per_unit_verbatim: a female with a BMI z-score of 0.50 who reallocates 30 min of any form of screen time with 30 min of physical activity at baseline would be estimated to have a BMI z-score of 0.47 at one-year follow-up
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion acknowledges that effect sizes are small and may not be clinically meaningful for individuals over one year, but argues they are important due to the widespread nature of screen time use and potential for accumulation. The paper is relatively measured in its conclusions, calling for experimental work before causal claims. It appropriately highlights sex differences and the dependency of associations on what screen time replaces.
